screens.subtitle: Weak or reused passwords make it easy for attackers to access your accounts. Strong password habits can prevent most unauthorized access.
agenda: Learn how to protect your accounts
Learn how to create strong passwords

screens.context: You are a new hire logging into your work accounts for the first time. You need to create a password for your company email and internal systems.

interactiveImage.imageAlt: Password creation screen showing security requirements

questions.question: You are creating a new password for your account. Which option is the strongest choice?

options.label: Password123!
options.description: NOT QUITE: This is extremely common and easily guessed by attackers using password lists.

options.label: Aaron2067._.
options.description: NOT QUITE: Predictable patterns like name and birth year are commonly targeted in attacks.

options.label: CompanyName67.
options.description: NOT QUITE: Using company or personal information makes passwords easier to guess.

options.label: G7$kL!2pQz321
options.description: CORRECT: Randomized and long combinations are much harder to crack and significantly more secure.

questions.question: You find our that on eof your passwords has been leaked in a data breach. What should you do?

options.label: Change your username but keep the same password.
options.description: NOT QUITE: Your username is public to people working within databases, your password is the compromised secret, therefore you should change the password instead

options.label: Change that password on this account and any other account using teh same password.
options.description: CORRECT: Reused passwords are DANGEROUS because attackers may try to use that password on your other accounts (which are connected by something common like your email).

options.label: Ignore the breach since you didn't do anything wrong.
options.description: NOT QUITE: Even if it wasn't your fault that your password got breached, it can still put you at risk.

options.label: Only change the password if you notice suspicious activity.
options.description: NOT QUITE: Waiting for the attacker to access your account often means it's too late!

questions.question: What is the best thing to pair with your password?

options.label: A password hint that makes your password easier to remember.
options.description: NOT QUITE: Hints can help attackers guess your password quicker.

options.label: The same password on all of your work accounts.
options.description: NOT QUITE: All accounts should ideally have separate and unique passwords

options.label: Multifactor authentication.
options.description: CORRECT: Using resources like Microsoft Authenticator / DUO adds an extra layer of protection to your accounts.

options.label: A shorter password that you will remember easily.
options.description: NOT QUITE: Convience usually comes at the cost of your personal security

screens.label: Clickable Password Breakdown

interactiveImage.imageAlt: Password options shown for users to inspect weak patterns

screens.instructions: Review each password and click on the parts that make it weak. As you tap, explanations will appear on the side.

clickableElements.label: Password
clickableElements.explanation: Common words like 'Password' are among the first combinations attackers test.
Simple number and symbol patterns are predictable and commonly used.

clickableElements.label: Aaron
clickableElements.explanation: Names are personal identifiers that make passwords easier to guess.
Years and birthday-like number patterns are often targeted in guessing attacks.

clickableElements.label: CompanyName
clickableElements.explanation: Company names or workplace-related words are easier to predict in a work setting.
Short, predictable number and punctuation patterns do not add much security.

clickableElements.label: Entire password
clickableElements.explanation: This password is long, randomized, and does not rely on personal or predictable information.

screens.title: Congrats on completing your module! Remember to...

cards.title: Take the extra mile and use unique passwords
cards.description: We know its tempting to constantly reuse passwords, but using a unique password for each account can protect all your other accounts even if one password gets leaked.

cards.title: Create passwords that consist of long, random patterns
cards.description: Randomized long patterns are much less likely to be guessed by attackers.

cards.title: Utilize multistep verification
cards.description: Even if your password gets leaked, multistep verification will protect your accounts.

screens.label: Module Complete
screens.reflection: Congrats on completing your interactive module!
takeaways: Common weak passwords and what to avoid
What makes a password strong and secure
Steps to take that will better protect your accounts

confidenceMeasure.question: How would you rate your confidence on this topic?
